title: Cooking Oil on Carpet
timeEstimate: 20–40 minutes; powder preload + Dawn wins most cases
stainChemistry: Cooking oil — vegetable / canola / olive / peanut / corn — is long-chain fat suspended by low viscosity. Low viscosity means cooking oil penetrates carpet fiber + pad rapidly — within minutes. Fresh oil is easily emulsified by Dawn. AGED cooking oil oxidizes and polymerizes into a varnish-like film, especially polyunsaturated oils (canola, soybean, corn) which lock together fastest; olive oil polymerizes slower; peanut oil is in between. Once polymerized, surface chemistry won't budge it — dry-side solvent needed. Carpet pad saturation is the other failure mode: pouring water / soap on oil drives the oil into the pad via the water carrier. Powder preload FIRST (no water) wicks most of the oil up before any chemistry.
whyThisWorks: Powder (cornstarch / baking soda / fuller's earth) wicks oil UP out of carpet by capillary action — no water, no heat, no risk. Dawn then emulsifies residual oil into water-soluble micelles. Aged / polymerized oil needs a solvent softening pass first (mineral spirits or Goo Gone on a cloth), then Dawn. Never pour water — water spreads oil and carries it into the pad.
defaultAssumption: Kitchen-floor splash, dinner-plate tip, olive-oil drizzle that missed. Fresh = quick win with powder + Dawn. Aged cooking oil on carpet that has been walked on or been through a dryer (if rug) = harder but still home-treatable with solvent softening first.

spottingProtocol.technique: Wick oil via capillary — no water, no chemistry
spottingProtocol.equipment: Cornstarch, baking soda, or fuller's earth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cover the oil stain with a generous layer of cornstarch, baking soda, or fuller's earth. Let sit 20–30 min. Powder wicks oil UP out of the fiber — no water needed. Vacuum off. Repeat with fresh powder if the first layer fully saturated.
Dawn in cool water on damp cloth. Work into spot with soft toothbrush. Emulsifies residual oil into water-soluble droplets. Damp-cloth rinse — NEVER pour water on carpet.

spottingProtocol.technique: Solvent soften for set oil film
spottingProtocol.equipment: Goo Gone or mineral spirits on white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: For aged oil with a stiff / shiny feel: Goo Gone or mineral spirits on a white cloth (never pour on carpet). Tamp from top, outside-in. Flip cloth. Ventilate. Follow with another Dawn pass.

Weighted dry towel 30 min. Fan-dry fully. Inspect in daylight. Residual grease feel = one more Dawn pass. Residual stain = peroxide unnecessary (oil is clear); may be dirt that wicked in — treat as separate dirt stain if visible.

steps: 1. Powder preload 20–30 min. Vacuum. Repeat if saturated.
2. Dawn / NSD + soft brush, damp cloth.
3. Aged / polymerized: Goo Gone / mineral spirits on cloth, tamp top.
professionalProtocol.temperature: room temp throughout; no heat until oil is cleared
warnings: NEVER pour water on carpet — water spreads oil + saturates pad
Powder preload cuts work in half
Flammable solvent for aged variant — ventilate, no flame

homeSolutions.agent: Powder First — Pull Oil Up Without Water
homeSolutions.instruction: Before you touch water or soap, cover the oil spot with cornstarch, baking soda, or plain baby powder. Let it sit at least 20–30 minutes. The powder wicks oil UP out of the carpet fiber through capillary action — no scrubbing, no chemistry. Vacuum off with a carpet attachment. Much of the stain usually comes with it. If the first layer turned visibly wet / saturated, dust on a fresh layer and repeat.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn — Generous, on a Damp Cloth
homeSolutions.instruction: Work a drop of Dawn in cool water into a damp cloth. Press and tamp on the spot with a soft toothbrush for a minute or two. Let it dwell 5 minutes. Dawn is engineered for exactly this — fat emulsion. Rinse with a damp cloth; NEVER pour water on the carpet. Water + oil = spreads to a bigger spot AND drives oil into the pad underneath.

homeSolutions.agent: Aged or Stiff-Feel Oil? Goo Gone or Mineral Spirits
homeSolutions.instruction: If the oil has been there long enough to feel stiff or shiny (polymerized), Dawn alone won't finish it. Apply Goo Gone (orange bottle) or mineral spirits to a clean white cloth — NEVER pour on carpet. Press and tamp the spot from the top, flip the cloth as it picks up oil. Work outside-in. Open windows — flammable. Then return to Dawn step to clear the solvent.

Stack dry paper towels on the spot, put a heavy book on top, 30 minutes. Fan-dry until the carpet is fully dry. Inspect in daylight. Feel with a clean fingertip — still oily? One more Dawn round. Fully dry = done.

If the stain stays after powder + Dawn + solvent, or if someone poured water on the spot before you got there (pad saturation), call a pro. Tell them: "Cooking oil on [nylon / polyester / wool] carpet, age [fresh / aged / polymerized], home-treated with powder preload + Dawn + [Goo Gone]. No water poured. [Residual oily feel / shiny film / pad suspected]."

steps: 1. Powder first — cornstarch / baking soda 20–30 min. Vacuum.
2. Repeat with fresh powder if first was saturated.
3. Dawn + cool water damp cloth + soft toothbrush, 5 min.
4. Aged / polymerized: Goo Gone / mineral spirits on RAG, tamp top.
6. NEVER pour water on the carpet — spreads oil + pad soak.
products: Goo Gone / mineral spirits (aged oil only)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if oil has polymerized and two rounds of Dawn + solvent don't clear it, or if pad is saturated (pro extraction).
warnings: NEVER pour water on carpet — water spreads oil + soaks pad
Flammable solvent for aged variant — ventilate, no flame

neverDo: Never pour water on carpet with oil
Never rub — drives oil into pad
fiberSensitivities: Nylon / polyester / olefin: tolerates full protocol

materialWarnings: Powder preload cuts work in half
Never pour water on an oil stain
Polyunsaturated oils polymerize faster than olive oil

escalation.when: Polymerized oil film after powder + Dawn + solvent, pad saturation suspected (someone poured water), or stain survives two home rounds.
escalation.whatToTell: "Cooking oil on [nylon / polyester / wool] carpet, age [fresh / aged / polymerized], home-treated with powder preload + Dawn + [Goo Gone / mineral spirits]. No water poured. [Residual oily film / pad saturation]."
escalation.specialistType: professional carpet cleaner with solvent spotting

contextQuestions: Type of oil — olive / canola / vegetable / peanut?
Has anyone poured water on the spot?
Has the area been walked on a lot since (grinds oil in)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Polyunsaturated oils (canola, soybean, corn) polymerize fastest. Olive oil is slower; extra-virgin even slower due to higher antioxidant content. If someone already poured water on a fresh oil stain, assume pad saturation and escalate — home chemistry won't reach pad oil.
